comments:
This paper presents an end-to-end MAP training criterion for hybrid HMM–DNN acoustic models that incorporates a simple (bi-gram) language model into training and allows standard WFST/HMM decoding at test time. The mathematical formulation is clearly presented: the authors derive forward/backward recursions, gradients for network and HMM parameters, and discuss numerical-stability and implementation details. The implementation in TensorFlow with WFST decoding and the reported small extra training cost (5–8%) relative to CTC make the approach practical. Experimental validation on WSJ shows consistent WER improvements over a comparable CTC baseline and qualitatively better alignments; the authors also study the effect of the training LM.

Strengths: clear principled derivation from a MAP perspective, computationally practical implementation, integration with WFST decoding, and promising WSJ results. The discussion of numerical issues and implementation choices is useful.

Weaknesses / Concerns: the provided excerpt omits concrete WER numbers in the main result table (only qualitative statements appear here), making it hard to judge the magnitude of the gains. The evaluation is limited to WSJ (81 hours); broader empirical validation (e.g., larger corpora such as LibriSpeech/Switchboard) and comparisons to other strong end-to-end and hybrid baselines would strengthen the claims. The approach relies on a training-time LM that is different (simpler) than the decoding LM; more analysis of the effect of LM mismatch and the sensitivity to LM order and quality is needed. Ablation studies (e.g., effect of pretraining q(c,c'), effect on convergence speed, quantitative alignment metrics) are missing. Some practical aspects — e.g., memory/graph size when composing WFSTs for large vocabularies and handling OOV tokens — are not fully discussed.

Overall, the work is technically sound and incremental: it provides a useful alternative to CTC for settings where stable alignments and WFST-based decoding are desired. I recommend acceptance as a contribution of moderate impact, contingent on fuller empirical reporting and more extensive comparisons in a final version (and ideally release of code to aid reproducibility).
